title: How to Track and Improve Technician Performance (Data-Driven Guide)
author: Mobibix
excerpt: Are your technicians generating profit or costing you money in parts breakage? Learn the core KPIs every repair shop owner must track to evaluate and incentivize staff.
content:
## Introduction

Your technicians are the engine of your repair shop. If the engine is running smoothly, your profits soar. If the engine is sputtering—breaking expensive parts, taking hours on simple jobs, or alienating customers with poor workmanship—your business will stall.

The problem in many Indian repair shops is that owners evaluate technicians entirely on "gut feeling" rather than data. You might think Technician A is your best employee because he works fast, without realizing his fast work results in a 15% warranty return rate.

Here are the critical Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) you must track digitally in 2026 to evaluate, train, and reward your technical staff.

---

## 1. Number of Completed Tickets (Velocity)

This is the baseline metric. How many devices does a technician successfully repair per week or month? 

While important, tracking raw volume without context is dangerous. A tech who does 50 simple battery swaps in a week will look better on a spreadsheet than a specialized tech who executes 10 highly complex motherboard IC replacements that generate significantly more profit.

**Best Practice:** Segment job volume by repair type (Level 1 vs Level 3 repairs) when evaluating velocity.

---

## 2. Average Turnaround Time (TAT) by Repair Type

If the shop average to replace an iPhone 13 screen is 45 minutes, but Technician B consistently takes 90 minutes, you have identified a training gap.

Your POS system should automatically measure the time from when a ticket status changes from "Assigned" to "Ready for Pickup." 

Monitoring this data allows you to identify which technicians excel at which tasks, allowing you to route incoming jobs defensively to the fastest tech for that specific repair category.

---

## 3. The Re-work Rate (Quality)

This is the single most critical metric. The Re-work Rate (or Return Rate) measures the percentage of a technician's jobs that return to the shop under warranty within 30 days due to failed parts or poor workmanship.

Fast work is useless if the customer comes back angry three days later because their screen is detaching from the frame.

If Technician A has a high velocity but a 12% re-work rate, they are actively damaging your brand reputation and burning through your spare parts margins. A great technician maintains a re-work rate well under 3%.

---

## 4. Parts Shrinkage / Breakage Rate

Did your tech "accidentally" snap an expensive display flex cable during installation? In a chaotic shop without digital tracking, they might quietly grab a second screen from the inventory bin without telling anyone to hide their mistake.

Your software must tie every consumed part to a specific technician ID. If a tech requires two screens to finish a single iPhone replacement job, the system logs the first part as "Waste/Damaged." 

Reviewing the "Waste Report" monthly allows you to pinpoint which technicians are costing you the most in broken components.

---

## Implementing a Performance Bonus Structure

Once you are tracking these metrics digitally, you can implement a foolproof incentive structure. Do not pay purely based on volume.

**Example Commission Structure:**
*   Base Salary.
*   + ₹100 bonus for every completed Level 2 repair.
*   **Penalty:** If a device returns for re-work due to poor installation, the technician loses the ₹100 bonus for that job, AND a ₹50 deduction from their overall pool to account for the wasted labor time to fix it.

This instantly aligns their financial incentives with your shop's reputation: Do it fast, but do it perfectly.

---

## Conclusion

You cannot manage what you do not measure. Moving from a completely manual shop to a software-driven operation allows you to stop guessing who your best employees are.
